findings.description: The escape sequence — from Lirien collapsing the scaffolding to the two women walking together — is the best action beat in Act I, but the subordinate's decision to abandon his prisoner to help his partner with a fallen market stall strains credibility slightly. This is a Conclave operative escorting a high-value detainee. Walking away to help untangle someone from canvas feels like a judgment error that trained personnel wouldn't make.
findings.location: Scene 2, paragraph 'The subordinate behind Kaelith made a decision. Stay here, he said'
findings.readerImpact: Most readers will accept it in the momentum of the scene. But a reader who thinks tactically (and this book trains readers to think tactically) may notice. A small addition — the subordinate seeing Seldric gesture for help, or an implied order — would ground the decision in chain-of-command obedience rather than independent incompetence.

findings.description: The warehouse scene at the end does important work (Kaelith reveals the dispatch contents, Lirien confirms the Warden implications, they establish the mutual-need alliance), but after the high tension of the escape, the pacing necessarily downshifts. This is structurally correct — you can't sustain escape-level tension indefinitely — but the warehouse dialogue covers some of the same ground the reader already knows from Ch. 2 (Pallor failing, Warden capacity, someone wants Kaelith dead).
findings.location: Scene 3, from 'They stopped in an abandoned warehouse' to end
findings.readerImpact: The reader who has absorbed Lirien's Ch. 2 intelligence knows the Warden/Pallor connection already. What's new here is Kaelith learning it and the two women sharing information — which is emotionally important. The scene works because of the character dynamics, not the information. It just has to earn its length through those dynamics rather than exposition.

findings.description: Kaelith's discovery of the dispatches happens because Tavik's office is coincidentally unlocked with classified materials left out on the desk. The text addresses this obliquely (Tavik left in a hurry, candle still burning), but doesn't fully explain why a compromised commander would leave Conclave encryption on his desk. This could be Tavik being careless, or it could be a planted discovery — but the narrative doesn't signal either interpretation.
findings.location: Scene 1, opening paragraphs establishing the unlocked office
findings.readerImpact: Minor. The dispatches' content is compelling enough that the reader moves past the convenience. But a sharp reader might wonder if Tavik left the door open deliberately (to warn Kaelith?) — and if so, that's a character beat worth signaling.

strengths: This is the engine chapter of Act I — everything shifts here. The pacing is excellent: tense infiltration → devastating revelation → immediate physical threat → forced alliance. Each scene escalates the stakes.

The dispatch documents are perfectly written. 'Maintenance schedule proceeds per previous instruction. Next refresh: 14 days.' The bureaucratic language makes the horror worse. The reader feels the institutional machinery grinding over a person.

'Warden-lineage capacity. Access to Quietus location. Execution remains undesirable.' — presented as a list, read by Kaelith in real time. The reader processes the implications alongside the character. This is how you do an exposition reveal without it feeling like exposition.

The scaffolding collapse is a brilliant intervention device — it communicates Lirien's skill, her choice, and her message ('I did that. This is a window. Use it.') in a single action without dialogue. The reader understands everything through blocking.

'I don't trust you.' / 'You shouldn't.' / 'But you don't have better options. And neither do I.' — This exchange is the beating heart of the enemies-to-lovers premise. Neither woman is pretending. Neither is yielding. They're building alliance on the only foundation available: mutual necessity. Romance readers will live for this.

The Calwrath reframe — Kaelith realizing her disgrace was manufactured as a containment mechanism — is emotionally devastating and narratively efficient. It retroactively charges everything in Ch. 1 with new meaning.
